text.title: Behavioral Design Patterns Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every behavioral-patterns application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Apply Strategy when you have three or more algorithm variants that swap at runtime AND the switching logic is a growing if/else or switch-on-type. With two variants, a simple conditional is cleaner.
rule.rationale: Premature abstraction with one or two implementations produces an interface with no real polymorphism; the Strategy indirection adds ceremony without extensibility benefit.

rule.statement: Use Observer for fan-out notification to N consumers where the subject does not need to know who is listening. Use direct callbacks or dependency injection when the relationship is one-to-one.
rule.rationale: Observer decouples the subject from consumers at the cost of making notification order and error propagation implicit. One-to-one relationships don't need that indirection.

rule.statement: Apply Command when you need undo/redo, operation queuing, or audit logging — not as a general wrapper for every method call. Command adds a class per operation; reserve it for operations where that overhead is justified.
rule.rationale: Wrapping every service method in a Command object creates an explosion of classes with no real benefit if the operations never need to be undone, queued, or replayed.

rule.statement: Apply State when an object's behavior changes significantly across multiple states AND the state-transition logic is growing into a complex conditional. With 2–3 states and simple transitions, an enum + switch is cleaner.
rule.rationale: State pattern promotes each state to a class; this is only worth the overhead when per-state behavior has real complexity. Simple enums with minimal branching don't justify the class explosion.

rule.statement: Use Chain of Responsibility for middleware pipelines where each handler decides to process the request or pass it on. Prefer a function-composition approach (list of handler functions iterated in order) over class-based chains for simple pipelines.
rule.rationale: Class-based chains add boilerplate for simple middleware cases; a list of handler functions achieves the same decoupling with less ceremony in languages with first-class functions.

rule.statement: Apply Template Method when multiple classes share the same algorithm structure but differ in steps. If algorithm structure itself varies, use Strategy instead.
rule.rationale: Template Method uses inheritance to fix the skeleton; Strategy uses composition to swap the whole algorithm. Inheritance is harder to test and refactor; prefer Strategy when both could work.

rule.statement: Use Mediator to reduce many-to-many coupling between components. Stop when the Mediator becomes a god object that knows too much — split into multiple mediators by domain.
rule.rationale: A Mediator that coordinates all components becomes a single point of failure and a maintainability bottleneck. The pattern solves coupling; one Mediator per domain keeps it bounded.

rule.statement: Apply Visitor when you need to add multiple operations to a stable class hierarchy without modifying those classes. If the hierarchy changes frequently, Visitor is the wrong choice — every new class requires updating all visitors.
rule.rationale: Visitor trades hierarchy extensibility for operation extensibility. It is the inverse of the Open-Closed trade-off: Visitor makes adding operations easy, but adding new element types hard.

rule.statement: Every behavioural-pattern adoption MUST be justified by a documented variance hotspot (file + function + observed variance). Pattern without variance hotspot is cargo-cult.
rule.rationale: Pattern overhead is justified by variance removal; without variance it adds indirection cost with zero benefit.

rule.statement: Pattern selection MUST come from the 9-pattern enum (Strategy, Observer, Command, State, Chain of Responsibility, Template Method, Mediator, Iterator, Visitor). Custom-named patterns are blocked.
rule.rationale: Closed enum lets reviewers and tooling reason about the codebase; custom names erode collective vocabulary.

rule.statement: Every selection record MUST include a one-sentence anti-misuse note naming the most-confusable alternative pattern and why it is NOT that one.
rule.rationale: Anti-misuse note forces the picker to articulate the choice; reduces Strategy/State and Observer/Mediator confusion.

rule.statement: Every concrete pattern implementation MUST have unit tests; the abstract has a contract test exercised by every concrete via shared fixtures.
rule.rationale: Pattern decay starts when concretes drift from the abstract contract; per-concrete tests + a contract test catch this immediately.

rule.statement: Observer pattern adoption MUST pair every register with an unregister and document the listener lifecycle (object scope, request scope, app scope).
rule.rationale: Forgotten listeners are the #1 cause of memory leaks in long-lived processes; the rule forces the pairing.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
